instance.name: santo_02

parts.label: Front Left Leg
parts.notes: Visible severe breakout halfway up the outer face.

parts.label: Front Right Leg
parts.notes: Crack running from top edge down approximately 8cm.

parts.label: Back Left Leg
parts.notes: Surface scratches mid-shaft; surface only, no structural impact.

parts.label: Back Right Leg
parts.notes: Large chip on top surface where it meets backrest joint.

parts.label: Seat Panel
parts.notes: Rough surface texture; lacquer worn through in central area.

parts.label: Backrest
parts.notes: Slated for removal under adaptive-reuse intent — not needed for planter form.

instance.provenance: Stacking chair, c. 1970s Central European industrial design. Light beech with clear lacquer. Owner is open to creative reuse rather than restoration to original form.
instance.notes: Owner has expressed that the chair has sentimental value but is no longer comfortable to sit on, and that turning it into a planter or low side-table would be a meaningful afterlife.

evidence.text: Walk-around inspection. Chair sits stable on its four gliders. All joints feel snug. Damage is concentrated in the legs (chips, cracks, scratches) and the seat surface (worn lacquer, rough texture). Backrest is mechanically sound but visually unremarkable.

hypotheses.description: Large chunk of wood broken off the top surface of the back right leg, near the backrest joint. Roughly 3cm x 2cm.
Severe breakout of wood mid-shaft on the outer face of the front left leg. Exposed grain visible. Structural fibers intact.
Crack running from the top edge of the front right leg downward, approximately 8cm. Visible but appears tight; may or may not extend through full thickness.
Rough surface texture across the central area of the seat; clear lacquer has worn through and timber is open-grained. Cosmetic; the panel itself is structurally sound.
Surface scratches in the middle of the outer face of the back left leg. Several parallel marks consistent with being dragged against a wall.

intent.summary: Adaptive reuse: convert this damaged chair into a planter or low side-table. Accept existing chips, scratches, and worn finish as visible patina rather than concealing them. Remove the backrest to free the form. Prioritize ecological reuse over preservation of original function. Aesthetic intervention is welcome — the result should look intentional, not like a failed repair.

constraints.tools_available: hand saw, chisel set, mallet, clamps, random-orbit sander (240/400 grit), cordless drill, sandpaper assortment
constraints.materials_available: linseed oil, beeswax, hide glue, scrap timber offcuts, jute twine, watertight liner (if planter), felt pads
constraints.allowed_operations: saw, sand, drill, oil, wax, glue, line
constraints.avoid_operations: no power saw, no synthetic finishes, no metal hardware not already on the chair
constraints.additional_constraints: Workshop session is a single afternoon; result should be functional by end of session, even if final finish takes longer to cure.

messages.content: This chair is no longer comfortable to sit on but I don't want to throw it out. I'd rather turn it into something useful — maybe a planter or low side-table. I'm fine with the damage being visible, even celebrated. Help me figure out what to do.

Adaptive reuse is a lovely direction here — and the damage you've described actually supports it. The chips, cracks, and worn finish all read as a history that's worth keeping visible rather than erasing. A few questions before we plan: do you want to keep the chair height (it could become a tall planter or bar-height side-table), or shorten the legs for a low planter? And: are you committed to removing the backrest, or could you imagine the backrest staying as a vertical accent?
Keep the height as is — the legs are part of what I like. Remove the backrest, yes. I want a clear, flat top.

Good. That gives us a clear target: a chair-height side-table or planter base, with the seat as the top surface. The backrest comes off cleanly at the joints with the back legs — no structural risk. The seat's rough finish can stay as character, or get sanded back and oiled. The leg damage stays visible. When you're ready, tap "Generate plan" below and I'll propose a sequence of steps.
